vulnerability.title: Add nodev Option to Non-Root Local Partitions
vulnerability.description: The nodev mount option prevents files from being interpreted as character or block devices. Legitimate character and block devices should exist only in the /dev directory on the root partition or within chroot jails built for system services. Add the nodev option to the fourth column of /etc/fstab for the line which controls mounting of any non-root local partitions.
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if ( ! ( { rpm --quiet -q kernel-core ;} && { rpm --quiet -q rpm-ostree ;} && { rpm --quiet -q bootc ;} && { ! rpm --quiet -q openshift-kubelet ;} && ([ -f /run/ostree-booted ] || [ -L /ostree ]) ) && ! ( [ -f /.dockerenv ] || [ -f /run/.containerenv ] ) ); then MOUNT_OPTION="nodev" # Create array of local non-root partitions readarray -t partitions_records < <(findmnt --mtab --raw --evaluate | grep "^/\w" | grep -v "^/proc" | grep "\s/dev/\w") # Create array of polyinstantiated directories, in case one of them is found in mtab readarray -t polyinstantiated_dirs < \ <(grep -oP "^\s*[^#\s]+\s+\S+" /etc/security/namespace.conf | grep -oP "(?<=\s)\S+?(?=/?\$)") # Define excluded non-local file systems excluded_fstypes=( afs autofs ceph cif

triage_decision.reason: Adding the nodev mount option to non-root local partitions is a straightforward configuration change in /etc/fstab that does not affect authentication, networking, or critical bootloader settings. It is unlikely to cause loss of SSH/console access and can be applied with a simple remount or reboot if needed.
triage_decision.estimated_impact: filesystems affected; filesystem mount option change

reasoning_messages.reasoning: Read /etc/fstab and locate all non‑root local partitions (mount point ≠ “/”). For each line, inspect the options column; if “nodev” is missing, insert it without duplicating existing options. Use run_cmd to execute sed‑i in‑place edits, reading the file first to avoid accidental overwrites. After modification, the system will apply the change on next mount or reboot.

review_feedback: The plan modifies /etc/fstab in-place with sed without backup and replaces empty or '-' option fields with only 'nodev', which can drop default mount options (e.g., rw, relatime). This may cause partitions to mount with incorrect options, leading to read‑only mounts or missing exec/dev permissions, potentially breaking services that rely on those partitions (e.g., /var, /home). Additionally, a malformed fstab line could prevent mount -a from succeeding, causing loss of non‑root filesystems.

previous_review_verdicts.feedback: The plan correctly targets /etc/fstab entries for non‑root local partitions and adds the nodev option, then applies the changes with mount -a. This will remediate the vulnerability if the filtering and edits are implemented correctly.
concerns: Replacing a '-' options field with only 'nodev' can drop default mount options (e.g., rw, relatime).
The description of the filtering logic for non‑local filesystems is vague; improper filtering could modify network or virtual filesystems.
No backup of /etc/fstab is performed before in‑place editing, which could risk system stability if an error occurs.
Using a simple sed command per line may introduce syntax errors if the options field contains spaces or commas.
suggested_improvements: Create a backup of /etc/fstab (e.g., cp /etc/fstab /etc/fstab.bak) before editing.
When the options field is '-', replace it with 'defaults,nodev' instead of just 'nodev' to preserve default mount behavior.
Implement more precise filtering (e.g., using awk to check the filesystem type against a whitelist of local types such as ext4, xfs, btrfs).
Use a single, well‑tested sed or awk command that safely inserts 'nodev' without duplicating it, handling empty fields and commas correctly.
Add error handling after mount -a to roll back the backup if remounting fails.
